The ledger was unseen, yet its pages rustled with every whispered truth and every forgotten favor. Reputation, in that place, stood not as a badge but as a living coin, minted by memory and spent by time.

Taro learned the power of a single decision when a merchant offered him a night’s profit to betray a rival. The short-term appetite glittered: a stolen bite from a fruit he did not grow and a tale he did not earn. He could tell the tale to bend markets, to tilt votes, to win a favor. Yet beyond the unseen ledger, the long arc of standing waited: a future where neighbors trusted the quiet man who would not lie, or feared the loud man who would not confess.

They chose to speak a small truth that day: a correction to a misread tale, a refrain of honesty whispered into a crowded room. The memory, kept by mothers and elders, grew durable—etched into shared memory, a pattern of trust that weathered every mischance.

Thus reputation became town law.
